# MAIN ARTICLE GLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPH Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jpcs GLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPHGLYPH # Oversized macroporous flower-like Cu9S8used as an efficient peroxidase mimetic enzyme for the degradation of organic dyes Ju Cheng a , Shouying Wu a , Linping Zhang a,b , Peiwen Zhou a , Yi Zhong a , Hong Xu a , Zhiping Mao a,c,* - a Key Lab of Science and Technology of Eco-textile, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, China - b Tsinghua University, Dept Chem, Ministry of Education, Key Lab Bioorganic Phosphorus Chem & Chem Biol, Beijing, 100084, China - c National Manufacturing Innovation Center of Advanced Dyeing and Finishing Technology, Tai ' an, Shandong, 271000, China # A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Macroporous structure Heterogeneous system Sacrificial template Peroxidase mimetic Copper # 1. Introduction With the rapid development of industrialization, organic dyes have been widely used in textile, printing, and leather, which are released to the environment along with wastewater. Many organic dyes are toxic and resistant to biodegradation. Among them, azo dyes that have antioxidation and anti-photodegradation properties are used widely and mostly in the dyeing and printing industry [1]. Therefore, it is significant to explore the methods to remove organic dyes from wastewater efficiently. There are numerous wastewater treatment technologies, such as ion exchange [2], adsorption [3], precipitation [4], biological treatment [5], photocatalytic [6], and membrane separation [7]. However, all of these methods have their limitations. Advanced oxidation process (AOPs) gets more attention in recent years due to their mild reaction conditions, strong oxidation ability, and wide application field [8]. As one of the most typical AOPs, the classic Fenton reaction is widely applied. During the Fenton reaction, hydroxyl radicals ( · OH) and superoxide radicals ( · O2GLYPH<0> ) are produced along with the catalyzation of # A B S T R A C T The development of a high-efficiency and stable heterogeneous catalyst has great significance for the recycling treatment of printing and dyeing wastewater. The flower-like Cu9S8with super large macroporous (d = 133.78nm) was prepared using porous CuO that was synthesized by the sacrificial template method. The peroxidase mimics activity of flower-like Cu9S8was determined by catalytic oxidation of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with H2O2. The kinetic constants (Km = 0.449mM) displayed that the flower-like Cu9S8showed excellent affinity to H2O2, which was8times lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The results of degradation experiments on different kinds of organic dyes showed that the Cu9S8had a wide application range in the degradation of different dyes with the degradation rate over98%, and the removal rate of total organic carbon reached78.2%. The flower-like Cu9S8can be a candidate catalyzer in the field of printing and dyeing wastewater treatment due to its high catalytic cycle stability and wide pH application range (3.0 -8.0). H2O2by iron ions. Some metal ion pairs (Cu + /Cu2 + , Fe2 + /Fe3 + , Co2 + /Co3 + ) also exhibited catalytic activity in the Fenton system due to their variable valence [9 -11]. However, metal ions in Fenton-like systems also have the following shortcomings: (1) Water-soluble catalysts are difficult to be recycled; (2) The applicable pH range is narrow which is easy to result in the sludge and secondary pollution; (3) Metal ions are harmful to the human body and the environment [12]. To solve the above problems, the study of heterogeneous peroxide mimic enzymes has become a hot research topic. But the catalytic activity of the heterogeneous catalyst is affected by its specific surface area, pore structure, surface composition, and redox potential. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have gained increasing attention by researchers due to their high specific surface area and microporous structure. Scientists have conducted a series of explorations on the application of MOFs in wastewater treatment [13 -15]. Subsequent studies have shown that MOFs are constructed by the microporous structure which can just allow a single dye molecule to pass. If the dye molecules aggregate, the aggregation will not be able to pass through the pores to bind to the internal active sites anymore. A porous material with larger pore size can * Corresponding author. No. 2999North Renmin Road, Shanghai, 201620, China. E-mail address: zhpmao@dhu.edu.cn (Z. Mao). Fig. 1. (a) XRD patterns of catalyst samples (Cu-MOF, CuO, flower-like Cu9S8, and irregular CuS); (b) XRD patterns of samples with different anion exchange times. Fig. 2. N2adsorption-desorption isotherms of Cu-MOF, CuO, and flower-like Cu9S8 (the inset shows pore size distribution curves of Cu-MOF, CuO, and flower-like Cu9S8).

SBET: Specific surface area by BET method; V: pore volume; dBET: Average pore diameter by BET method; dBJH: Average pore diameter by BJH method. be obtained by removing the organic frameworks of MOFs under high-temperature condition [16]. Compared with iron-based catalysts (Fe(III)/Fe(II)), copper-based catalysts (Cu(II)/Cu(I)) have more abundant active sites, better acid and alkali resistance, and higher conversion rate [17]. CuS exhibits excellent stability and catalytic activity in heterogeneous catalytic systems contributed by its low solubility and variable valence properties. According to the previous reports, CuS can form a variety of nanocrystalline morphologies, such as nano quantum dots [18], nanorods [19], nanotubes [20], nanosheets [21], and nanospheres [22]. The CuS nanomaterials with high specific surface area and large pore size are rarely used in heterogeneous catalytic systems. Herein, the flower-like Cu9S8with super-large macroporous structure was obtained by a sacrificial template method and applied to the Fenton-like system to degrade organic dyes. The obtained flower-like Cu9S8not only showed excellent peroxidase mimicking activity (Km value of0.449with H2O2as the substrate is8times lower than horseradish peroxidase (HRP)), but also the high degradation rate of MB (97%) after10catalysis cycles. Flower-like Cu9S8can be employed in a wide pH range in the heterogeneous catalytic system. # 2. Experimental section # 2.1. Materials Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2 · 3H2O, AR), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2 · 2H2O, AR), sodium sulfide (Na2S, AR), 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, AR) ， tert-butanol (TBA, AR), ascorbic acid (AA, AR), rhodamine B (Rh B, AR) ， methylene blue (MB, AR), acetic acid, sodium acetate, methanol, ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1,3,5-Benzene tricarboxylic acid (H3BTC, AR), 30% of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium laurate (SL, AR) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Reactive blue13 (RB13), reactive blue19 (RB19), reactive red2 (RR2), reactive red195 (RR195), acid orange7 (AO7), acid red1 (AR1) were obtained from Shanghai Annoqi Group Co., Ltd. Commercial copper sulfide (CuS) was obtained from Shanghai MacLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. # 2.2. Preparation of Cu-MOF, CuO, and flower-like Cu9S8 Cu (NO3)2 · 3H2O (5.0430g) was dissolved in methanol (100mL). H3BTC (5.7984g), CTAB (0.3645g), and SL (0.2222g) were dissolved in methanol (100mL). Under vigorous stirring, the Cu (NO3)2 · 3H2O solution was added dropwise to the latter at a rate of2mL/min. Then the mixture was stirred for30min and aged at room temperature for24h. Finally, the collected sol was centrifuged at9000rpm for4min, washed three times with methanol, and dried at60 ◦ C for4h to obtain Cu-MOF. Porous CuO was prepared by the calcination of Cu-MOF. The Cu-MOF was placed on an alumina crucible and then heated at450 ◦ C in a muffle furnace for2h at a heating rate of2 ◦ C/min in an air atmosphere. CuO was transformed into flower-like Cu9S8via a facile anion exchange method. 10mg of CuO was dispersed in10mL deionized water, and the mixed dispersion was ultrasonically dispersed to form a uniform sol. Fig. 3. SEM images of samples: (a, d) Cu-MOF; (b, e) CuO; (c, f) flower-like Cu9S8; (g) irregular CuS; (h) commercial CuS. Fig. 4. TEM images of samples: (a, b) CuO; (c, d) flower-like Cu9S8. Then, under vigorous stirring, 20mL Na2S (20mM) aqueous solution was poured into the above-mentioned CuO homogeneous sol. The reaction was carried out at room temperature for8h. Finally, the collected sol was centrifuged at9000rpm for4min, washed with ultrapure water, ethanol, and vacuum dried at60 ◦ C for4h. As a comparison, the CuS powder with irregular morphology was Fig. 5. SEM images of flower-like Cu9S8with different anion exchange time: (a) 0min; (b) 10min; (c) 30min; (d) 2h; (e) 4h; (f) 8h. Fig. 6. XPS spectra of flower-like Cu9S8before and after catalysis:(a) survey spectrum of flower-like Cu9S8particles before catalysis; (b) Cu2p spectrum of flowerlike Cu9S8before catalysis; (c) survey spectrum of flower-like Cu9S8after catalysis; (d) S2p spectrum of flower-like Cu9S8before catalysis. obtained by adding0.1mol L GLYPH<0> 1CuCl2dropwise to0.1mol L GLYPH<0> 1Na2S aqueous solution and stirring at room temperature for15min. Subsequently, the CuS powder was obtained after the centrifugation at9000rpm for4min. The precipitate was then washed three times with deionized water and ethanol respectively. The product was dried at60 ◦ C for4h to obtain irregular CuS. # 2.3. Characterization This X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained with an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (D/max-2550PC) with a copper K α ( λ = 1.54056 Å) X-ray source at diffraction angles (2 θ ) 5 -70 ◦ . The microscopic morphology of the sample was measured by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Regulus8230, Hitachi), working at5kV and10 μ A. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was measured on the JEM-2100transmission electron microscope (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by Escalab250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher, USA). N2adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured by a physical adsorption instrument (BET, V-Sorb2800P, Beijing Jinaipu). The total organic carbon (TOC) content was measured by the total organic carbon analyzer multi-N/C3100 (Jena, Germany). # 2.4. The catalyzed degradation of organic pollutants by H2O2 Generally, 20mg of catalyst, was added to50mL of organic dye aqueous solution (0.1mM) for ultrasonic dispersion for5min, and then the sol was stirred at50 ◦ C to form a homogeneous sol. The system was kept in a constant temperature oil bath for15min to reach adsorption equilibrium before catalysis. Then, the H2O2aqueous solution was added to the previous sol to catalyze the degradation of the dye. At each given time interval, 4mL of aliquot was taken and filtered through a Fig. 7. The effect of samples on the degradation rate of MB. Condition: 0.1mM MB, 0.4g L GLYPH<0> 1catalysts, 60mM H2O2, 50 ◦ C. 0.22 μ m nylon filter needle. The filtrate was detected by ultraviolet -visible spectroscopy (UV-1800, Shimadzu) to determine the degree of degradation of organic dyes. The effects of reaction temperature, pH value, and H2O2dosage on the catalytic conditions were investigated respectively. The catalyst was recovered by centrifugation, and the resulting precipitate was washed twice with deionized water and reused in the next catalytic cycle. # 2.5. Kinetic analysis of the flower-like Cu9S8catalyzed TMB oxidation by H2O2 The kinetic analysis of flower-like Cu9S8peroxidase mimetic enzyme was studied by monitoring the absorbance value at652nm after the first5min. Based on the traditional dual-substrate reaction, the concentration of one substrate was fixed and the concentration of the other substrate was changed for determination. Kinetic analysis of TMB (substrate): the concentration of H2O2was fixed at0.1mM, and a series of concentrations (0.1, 0.125, 0.167, 0.25, 0.5mM) of TMB was implemented and vice versa. The kinetic parameters were calculated by the following equations: Where v is the initial reaction rate, Vmax is the maximum reaction rate, A is the absorbance value at652nm, b is the thickness of the absorption layer1cm, and ε is the molar absorption coefficient of the oxidation product oxTMB of TMB39000M GLYPH<0> 1cm GLYPH<0> 1 , [S] is the substrate concentration, Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant, which approximates the affinity between the peroxidase mimetic enzyme and the substrate. The smaller the Km value, the stronger the affinity between the peroxidase mimetic enzyme and the substrate. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1. Characterization of the catalysts The crystal structure of catalyst samples was characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1a. The Cu-MOF sample has a sharp diffraction peak as shown in previous literature [23], indicating that Cu-MOF was successfully synthesized in this work. For CuO, the six diffraction peaks correspond to the (110), (1,1,-1), (111), (2,0,-2), (202) and (113) crystal planes of CuO (PDF#48-1548), indicating the successful preparation of CuO. Along with the anion exchange, CuO gradually transforms into Cu9S8. In the pattern of flower-like Cu9S8, the diffraction peaks of CuO completely disappeared. With increasing the time of the sulfuration reaction (as shown in Fig. 1b), the six diffraction peaks belonging to the CuO crystal gradually weaken, and a new peak at2 θ = 47.86 ◦ corresponds to the (111) lattice plane of digenite (Cu9S8) appeared. Finally, all the peaks of CuO disappeared and new peaks of Cu9S8left. These results implied that the CuO particles gradually transformed into Cu9S8particles with the progression of anion exchange. Instead, six new diffraction peaks appearing at2 θ = 27.72 ◦ , 29.15 ◦ , 32.32 ◦ , 47.86 ◦ , 52.84 ◦ and58.68 ◦ are assigned to (0,0,21), (0,0,22), (1,0,13), (111), (1, 1,16) and (1,1,24) lattice planes of Cu9S8 (PDF#36-0379). The results show that Cu9S8is formed after the anion exchange. As a comparison the irregular CuS powder was also characterized by XRD, the peak at2 θ = 31.78 ◦ can be attributed to the (103) lattice plane of CuS (PDF#06-0464), indicating that CuS powders are successfully synthesized. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of samples are investigated (Fig. 2). The three samples display a typical Fig. 8. (a) Double reciprocal plots of activity of flower-like Cu9S8with a fixed concentration of TMB (0.1mM) and varied concentration of H2O2; (b) double reciprocal plots of activity of flower-like Cu9S8with a fixed concentration of H2O2 (0.1mM) and varied concentration of TMB.

IV isotherm with an obvious hysteresis in the range of ca. 0.2 -1.0P/P0, indicating the existence of a mesoporous structure. The BrunauerEmmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of Cu-MOF is1279.35m2g GLYPH<0> 1 (as shown in Table1), and the pore size is concentrated at3.5nm. For CuO, the pore size distribution curve is approximately straight, which may be due to the disorder collapse of the material during the calcination process. Compared with CuO, the specific surface area and average pore diameter of flower-like Cu9S8increase to6.29m2g GLYPH<0> 1and133.78nm (as shown in Table1), respectively. Fig. 2 (the inset) shows that the pore size of flower-like Cu9S8is concentrated at55.7nm and103.7nm. This phenomenon is mainly due to that the rate of outward migration of Cu2 + ions is higher than the inward migration of S2ions during the anion exchange reaction process [12]. In addition, The super-large mesoporous structure of flower-like Cu9S8can facilitate the transfer of substrates and provide more active sites [24]. Fig. 3depicts the SEM images of samples. Fig. 3a and d shows that a diamond-shaped Cu-MOF was successfully synthesized. Under the hightemperature air atmosphere, the organic ligand in Cu-MOF is removed, and the microporous structure collapses disorderly, thereby forming the diamond-shaped CuO (Fig. 3b and e) with a rough surface and mesoporous structure. For Cu9S8 (Fig. 3c and f), it is a flower-like microsphere composed of nanosheets with thickness about30nm and width about1 μ m. As shown in Fig. 3g and h, commercial CuS and irregular CuS both show irregular shapes. Transmission electron microscopy technology is used to further explore the microstructure of CuO and Cu9S8. As shown in the high-resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) of Fig. 4a and b, the lattice fringe of the sample is determined to be0.28nm corresponding to the (110) crystal plane of CuO. Fig. 4c and d shows the structure of flower-like Cu9S8nanosheets. The lattice fringe spacing is0.30nm, which corresponds to the (0,0,22) plane of Cu9S8. Therefore, CuO is completely transformed into flower-like Cu9S8. Fig. 5shows the SEM images of Cu9S8with different anion exchange times during the treatment of CuO particles with Na2S solution. The vulcanization reaction occurs on the surface of diamond-shaped CuO to form the Cu9S8due to its solubility (Ksp = 6.3 × 10GLYPH<0> 36 ) being lower than CuO (Ksp = 2.2 × 10GLYPH<0> 20 ). The formation path of the three-dimensional spherical flower-like Cu9S8is consistent with the previous literature reports [25]. In the first10min, many small particles appear on the surface of the particles. According to the Ostwald ripening mechanism, the nanoparticles continued to grow with increasing the reaction time. After30min, nanosheets with a length of about100nm are formed due to small particles aggregating tighter to form some basic nanosheets. As the reaction goes on, the nanosheets grow bigger with a width of about600nm after2h. Eventually, flower-like Cu9S8, with the three-dimensional structure is constructed by the intersection of nanosheets. At the same time, XRD results (as shown in Fig. 1b) show the change of sample crystal type during this process. The valence states of the elements on the surface of the sample were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). For the highresolution XPS spectrum of Cu2p (Fig. 6b), two bands of931.7eV and934.3eV are attributed to Cu(I) 2p3/2and Cu(II) 2p3/2, respectively. In addition, the fitted bond energies of951.9eV and953.4eV should be identified as Cu(I) 2p1/2and Cu(II) 2p1/2, respectively [17]. In the S2p spectrum (Fig6d), 161.8eV and163.2eV are corresponded to S(II) 2p3/2and S(II) 2p1/2, respectively. At the same time, the two peaks of164.1eV and169.2eV are attributed to S2p and satellite peaks, respectively [26]. After the catalytic reaction, the valence state of copper on the surface of flower-like Cu9S8doesn ' t change. The flower-like Cu9S8will exhibit excellent catalytic ability due to the existence of Cu (I) and Cu(II). # 3.2. The peroxidase mimetic activity of flower-like Cu9S8 The samples collected at different stages and in different ways were used as heterogeneous peroxidase mimetic enzymes to catalyze the degradation of organic pollutants by H2O2, and the results were shown in Fig. 7. After15min balance adsorption before the addition of H2O2, the concentration of MB only decreased2%, because Cu9S8cannot provide much effective contact area (SBET = 6.29m2g GLYPH<0> 1 ) to absorb MB. When there is only H2O2without any catalyst or only flower-like Cu9S8without H2O2, the degradation rate of MB can be neglected. However, in the presence of catalyst and H2O2at the same time, MB concentration decreased significantly, and flower-like Cu9S8decreased most obviously, followed by commercial CuS and irregular CuS. The average pore diameter of commercial CuS and irregular CuS is smaller than flowerlike Cu9S8 (as shown in Table1). This is because when irregular CuS is formed, there is no template and there is only a separate nucleation and growth process. Thus, large-scale aggregation occurs between the particles resulting in the reduction of active sites that can catalyze the reaction. In addition, the peroxidase mimetic activity of the products at different stages is compared by monitoring the absorbance value of the maximum absorption wavelength (662nm) of MB. The concentration of MB catalyzed by the product obtained from the different stages decreases along with the reaction proceeding. The results show that the flower-like Cu9S8exhibits excellent activity in catalytic oxidation of MB by H2O2. Compared with Cu-MOF, CuO has a smaller specific surface area, but its average pore size is increased to15.58nm. The large pore size is more conducive to the combination of substrate and catalyst, thereby enhancing the effect of catalytic H2O2oxidation of MB. The flower-like Cu9S8showed strong peroxidase activity due to the large average pore size. # 3.3. Steady-state kinetic assay The kinetic parameters of the flower-like Cu9S8peroxidase mimics were obtained by varying one substrate concentration while fixing another substrate concentration. The steady-state kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax) were obtained by fitting the slope and intercept in the double reciprocal plot of the reaction rate and substrate concentrations (as shown in Fig. 8). Among them, the Vmax value represents reactivity. As shown in Table2, when H2O2is used as the substrate, the Km value of flower-like Cu9S8is2which is479times lower than other horseradish peroxidase mimics. The Km value of flower-like Cu9S8 (0.449mM) with H2O2as the substrate is8times lower than natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), indicating that flower-like Cu9S8has a higher affinity for H2O2than HRP or else metal-based peroxidase. On the other hand, the Km value of using TMB as a substrate of flower-like Cu9S8 (0.660mM) is slightly higher than natural horseradish peroxidase, which indicates that Fig. 9. (a) MB degradation curves of the heterogeneous system catalyzed by the Cu9S8at different H2O2concentrations; (b) MB degradation curves of the heterogeneous system catalyzed by the Cu9S8at different temperatures; (c) MB degradation curves of the heterogeneous system catalyzed by the Cu9S8at different pH values; (d) reusability of Cu9S8for degradation of MB; (e) XRD patterns of Cu9S8before and after10cycles; (f) SEM image of Cu9S8after10cycles. a larger concentration of TMB is needed to obtain the maximum reaction speed for flower-like Cu9S8. Based on the above results, the high peroxidase activity of flower-like Cu9S8can be explained as follows: The specific surface and large average pore diameter of flower-like Cu9S8provide a basic way to expose the active sites to the substrate as much as possible, and more catalytic sites are provided for excellent peroxidase activity. # 3.4. Catalytic property of Cu9S8 The synthesized Cu9S8was used as solid catalysts in the heterogeneous system for the degradation of organic pollutants. As shown in Fig. 9a, the degradation rate of MB has been significantly improved by increasing the concentration of H2O2from40mM to120mM. This is mainly attributed to that H2O2is the oxidant in the system, which provides the active substrates · OH and · O2GLYPH<0> . As the concentration of H2O2increased from40mM to60mM, the production rate was enhanced. With a further increase of the H2O2concentration (from60mM to120mM), the improvement of the MB decolorization effect was slight. This phenomenon can be explained by the following two points: (1) H2O2is also a consumer of · OH which can reduce the amount of · OH [46]; (2) the instability of excessive · OH leads to self-combination reactions [47]. Therefore, the concentration of H2O2is optimized, and the final concentration is determined to be60mM. As shown in Fig. 9b, the degradation rate of MB was significantly increased with the increase of the temperature from40 ◦ C to60 ◦ C. Under50 ◦ C, more than99% MB was removed within40min, which may be due to · OH produced by the mutual promotion of flower-like Cu9S8and H2O2. Firstly, when the temperature is low, the hydrogen peroxide in the solution produces · OH at a slower rate, and the degradation rate slows down. When the reaction temperature increases, the activation energy of each reactant also increases, thereby accelerating the production of · OH. Secondly, the movement between the molecules becomes violent which increases the chance of collision and contact between · OH and MB, further accelerating the rate of degradation reaction. However, the increase in temperature accelerates the direct decomposition of H2O2, resulting in the low utilization efficiency of H2O2 [48]. Finally, the temperature was determined to be50 ◦ C to achieve the best catalytic activity and economic cost. As shown in Fig. 9c, the catalytic activity of the catalyst under different pH conditions was explored. The results show that the Fig. 10. The UV -vis absorption spectra of different organic dyes:(a) RB19; (b) RR2; (c) AO7; (d) MB; (e) Rh B; (f) AR1; (inset shows). Insets depicts the timedependent photographs of different organic dyes solution treated by H2O2 + Cu9S8:( ⅰ ) RB19; ( ⅱ ) RR2; ( ⅲ ) AO7; ( ⅳ ) MB; ( ⅴ ) Rh B; ( ⅵ ) AR1. Condition: 0.1mM organic dyes,0.4g L GLYPH<0> 1Cu9S8, 60mM H2O2, 50 ◦ C. Fig. 11. (a) Catalytic mechanism study: the degradation rate of MB with different scavengers; (b) TOC values of MB or RR195 (0.1mM) solutions catalyzed by flower-like Cu9S8at50 ◦ C. catalyst can maintain excellent catalytic activity in a wide pH range from3.0to8.0. Under acidic conditions, it is beneficial to consume hydroxyl ions to produce · OH and promote the degradation of MB [49]. In addition, a series of catalytic cycle experiments have also been carried out to verify the stability of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 9d, no significant reduction in catalytic efficiency was observed for the catalyst after5cycles. Additionally, after10cycles, the catalytic effect of flowerlike Cu9S8reduces by2%. XRD pattern and SEM image (Fig. 9e and f) show that flower-like Cu9S8could still maintain its morphology and crystal phase after10cycles. Therefore, flower-like Cu9S8has not only excellent catalytic activity but also excellent recyclability. The recyclability of flower-like Cu9S8can be attributed to the following reasons: (1) The solubility of Cu9S8 (Ksp = 6.3 × 10GLYPH<0> 36 ) is low, so it is stable in water; (2) On the other hand, previous studies have shown that when the shape is spherical, it has better cycle stability [21,50]. As shown in Fig. 10, under the same conditions, the degradation to different dyes, such as reactive dyes, cationic dyes, and acid dyes, the general applicability of the synthesized flower-like Cu9S8was evaluated. Flower-like Cu9S8could decolorize all dyes except MB within21min, and the removal ratio is up to98%. For MB, it could achieve the99.7% decolorization ratio within40min. The experimental results show that the flower-like Cu9S8has an outstanding decolorization effect for different kinds of dyes in a relatively short time. # 3.5. Catalytic mechanism of Cu9S8 To study the catalytic degradation process, some free radical quenchers were added to the catalytic reaction system to capture free radicals to determine the active oxygen species, and the results are shown in Fig. 11a. As a scavenger of · OH radicals, there was a76% decrease after introducing the tert-butanol (TBA). Ascorbic acid, a specific scavenger of · O2GLYPH<0> radicals, could cause a15% loss of catalytic activity. The above results indicate that the · OH radicals were the main active oxygen species in the process of catalytic removal to MB. In addition, the catalytic removal experiments for two dyes (MB and RR195) were monitored by total organic carbon (TOC) content determination, and the results were shown in Fig. 11b. MB can finish the decolorization within40min, and its TOC curve shows that the total organic carbon content also decreases rapidly and finally dropped to27.2% after3h. There is also a similar result for the RR195, and the TOC value can drop to21.8% after3h. This provides strong evidence that the degradation process of organic dyes catalyzed by flower-like Cu9S8is not only a simple decolorization reaction but a carbonization reaction. Based on all the information obtained above, a possible mechanism of the activation of H2O2by flower-like Cu9S8is proposed as following parts: (1) The flower-like Cu9S8obtained by the sacrificial template method has a high specific surface area and a large pore size, which provides more reactive sites for the Fenton-like reaction to generate more hydroxyl radicals [12]. In addition, the porous structure facilitates the contact between the organic dye molecules and reactive sites inside the catalyst [51]. (2) According to the well-known Haber-Weiss cycle, Cu(I) can combine with H2O2to produce Cu(II) and hydroxyl radicals. Then, the generated Cu(II) can be reduced to Cu(I) by reacting with H2O2, and at the same time, · O2GLYPH<0> and H + are produced. In addition, the reaction constant of the conversion of Cu(I) to Cu(II) is much higher than Cu(II) to Cu(I). Therefore, the presence of Cu(I) on the flower-like Cu9S8surface accelerates the production rate of hydroxyl radicals. # 4. Conclusions In summary, the flower-like Cu9S8was successfully prepared by the sacrificial template method and applied to the degradation of different organic dyes. The prepared flower-like Cu9S8exhibits high peroxidase activity, and excellent affinity for H2O2, with Km value of0.449 (mM) which is8times lower than that of HRP. In the reaction system formed by catalysts, H2O2, and organic dyes, · OH radicals are the main active oxygen species. The degradation rates of different dyes are up to98%, and the degradation process is not only a simple decolorization reaction but a70 -80% complete carbonization process. Flower-like Cu9S8not only has excellent cycle stability but also a wide pH application range of3.0 -8.0. Therefore, the flower-like Cu9S8designed in this study has great prospects in acidic, neutral, or weak alkaline wastewater treatments. At the same time, it also provides data support and a theoretical basis for further studying the heterogeneous catalytic systems of copper-based catalysts. # Author statement Ju Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing-Original Draft; Shouying Wu: Data Curation, Software; Linping Zhang: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing-Review & Editing; Peiwen Zhou: Visualization, Supervision; Yi Zhong: Resources; Hong Xu: Methodology; Zhiping Mao (Corresponding Author): Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing; # Declaration of competing interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. # Acknowledgments This work is supported by National Key R & D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1801502) and the International Cooperation Fund of Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (21130750100). # References 1.
